large_adversarial.name: reverse_sorted
large_adversarial.why_works: First element is always max, creating 0 vs N-1 partition splits every level. Forces O(n²) comparisons.
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: Merge sort splits at midpoint regardless. Python sorted() uses Timsort which detects descending runs.

large_adversarial.name: median_of_three_killer
large_adversarial.construction_algorithm:
candidate = list(range(N))
killer = [0]*N
for i in range(N//2):
    killer[2*i] = candidate[i]
    killer[2*i+1] = candidate[N-1-i]
return killer
large_adversarial.why_works: Forces median-of-three pivot to always select near-min/max, causing maximally unbalanced partitions.
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: Introsort (C++ std::sort) detects depth limit and switches to heapsort. Merge sort unaffected.

O(n log n).reasoning: 10^5 * log(10^5) ≈ 1.7M ops per case, T=10 → 17M ops → ~0.03s for optimal. O(n²) at N=10^5 → 10^10 ops → TLE

O(n²).reasoning: 2000² = 4M ops per case, manageable. O(n³) at N=2000 → 8×10^9 → TLE

suboptimal_algorithms.name: Recursive DFS without stack size increase
suboptimal_algorithms.how_common: students who know DFS but not iterative version

suboptimal_algorithms.name: Adjacency matrix O(V²) scan
suboptimal_algorithms.how_common: students using matrix instead of adjacency list

large_adversarial.name: bamboo_chain
large_adversarial.construction_algorithm:
edges = [(i, i+1) for i in range(1, N)]
# N nodes, N-1 edges forming a line
# Query: traverse from node 1 to node N
large_adversarial.why_works: Recursion depth equals N. Python default limit is 1000. Even with sys.setrecursionlimit, stack memory exhausts at N=10^5.
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: Iterative BFS/DFS uses explicit stack data structure, O(V+E), unaffected by depth.

large_adversarial.name: complete_graph
large_adversarial.construction_algorithm:
N = 3000
edges = [(i,j) for i in range(1,N+1) for j in range(i+1,N+1)]
# ~4.5M edges
large_adversarial.why_works: O(V²) matrix scan: 3000² = 9M ops minimum. Adjacency list BFS: O(V+E) = same order but smaller constant.
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: Adjacency list processes each edge once in BFS. Total work O(V+E).

large_adversarial.name: maximum_overlapping_subproblems
large_adversarial.construction_algorithm:
# For LCS: two identical strings
s1 = 'a' * N
s2 = 'a' * N
# For knapsack: items with weight[i]=1, value[i]=1, capacity=N//2
# For coin change: coins=[1,2], target=N
large_adversarial.why_works: Every subproblem is recomputed exponentially many times without memoization.
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: Bottom-up DP computes each state exactly once. Total states × transition cost.

suboptimal_algorithms.name: Naive O(N*M) substring search
suboptimal_algorithms.how_common: students who don't know KMP or Z-function

large_adversarial.name: fibonacci_string
large_adversarial.construction_algorithm:
def fib_str(k):
    a, b = 'a', 'b'
    for _ in range(k-2):
        a, b = b, b+a
    return b
text = fib_str(k+4)
pattern = fib_str(k)
large_adversarial.why_works: Highly periodic with no clean period. Naive search restarts at every mismatch causing O(NM) total comparisons.
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: KMP failure function handles periodic patterns in O(N+M).

large_adversarial.name: all_same_char_late_mismatch
large_adversarial.construction_algorithm:
text = 'a' * (N-1) + 'b'
pattern = 'a' * (M-1) + 'b'
# Place so pattern almost-matches every position
large_adversarial.why_works: Every alignment: M-1 successes then 1 failure. Total: O(N*M) comparisons.
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: KMP skips matched prefix on mismatch using failure function.

suboptimal_algorithms.name: Nested loop O(N²)
suboptimal_algorithms.how_common: students who don't recognize two-pointer applicability

large_adversarial.name: answer_at_extremes
large_adversarial.construction_algorithm:
# For pair sum = target:
# Place valid pair at index 0 and N-1
arr = [non_valid_values_in_middle]
arr[0] = X
arr[N-1] = target - X
# Nested loop hits it last, two pointers hit it first
large_adversarial.why_works: Nested loop iterates all N² pairs before finding the last one. Two pointers converge to extremes in O(N).
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: Two pointers start at both ends by design.

suboptimal_algorithms.name: Recomputing sum for every query O(Q*N)
suboptimal_algorithms.how_common: students who don't precompute prefix array

large_adversarial.name: maximum_range_queries
large_adversarial.construction_algorithm:
N = 100000
Q = 100000
# All queries are full range [1, N]
queries = [(1, N)] * Q
large_adversarial.why_works: Each query recomputes sum of entire array. Q*N = 10^10 ops → TLE.
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: Prefix array answers each query in O(1). Q queries = O(Q).

large_adversarial.name: interleaved_insert_extract
large_adversarial.why_works: Re-sorting after each insert is O(N log N). Total: O(N² log N). Heap maintains order in O(log N) per op.
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: Heap insert/extract both O(log N). N operations = O(N log N) total.

suboptimal_algorithms.name: Backtracking without pruning
suboptimal_algorithms.how_common: students who implement recursion without constraint propagation

large_adversarial.name: no_solution_exists
large_adversarial.construction_algorithm:
# N-Queens: use N=15 with a board configuration that has no solution path pruned early
# Forces full tree exploration
N = 15
large_adversarial.why_works: Without pruning, explores all N! permutations. With pruning, most branches cut early. No-solution case forces maximum exploration.
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: Pruning cuts invalid branches at depth 1-2, reducing to manageable subtrees.

suboptimal_algorithms.name: Union Find without path compression
suboptimal_algorithms.complexity: O(N) per find in worst case
suboptimal_algorithms.how_common: students who implement basic union without optimization

large_adversarial.name: long_union_chain
large_adversarial.construction_algorithm:
# Union in sequence: union(1,2), union(2,3), union(3,4)... union(N-1,N)
# Then query find(1) — must traverse entire chain
for i in range(1, N): print(f'union {i} {i+1}')
print(f'find 1')
large_adversarial.why_works: Chain creates a tree of depth N-1. find(1) traverses all N nodes without path compression. O(N) per query × Q queries = O(NQ).
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: Path compression makes find() O(α(N)) ≈ O(1) amortized.

suboptimal_algorithms.name: Brute force O(N²) scan for next greater/smaller
suboptimal_algorithms.how_common: students who don't know monotonic stack trick

large_adversarial.name: full_stack_clear_at_end
large_adversarial.construction_algorithm:
# Strictly increasing then one very large value at end
# arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, ..., N-1, 10^9]
# Forces O(N) stack clear at last element
arr = list(range(1, N)) + [10**9]
large_adversarial.why_works: Naive O(N²) scans right for each element. N-1 elements have answer at index N-1, causing N² total comparisons.
large_adversarial.optimal_safe: Monotonic stack processes each element at most twice (push + pop). O(N) total.
